segments.s305-g89: هم اینجام کشت و هم اینجام خورد is a balanced, repetitive phrase: هم + اینجام places emphasis on location ('here as well'). کشت is a noun (cultivation/planting) used in parataxis with خورد (from خور, 'to eat'); there is no finite verb—Persian poetry often strings nouns or infinitive-stems together to list states or activities. The second هم + اینجام repeats the locative emphatically, linking the two activities.
segments.s305-g90: هم اینجام مردان روز نبرد continues the same locative pattern: هم اینجام (‘here too’) + مردان (plural noun, 'men/warriors'). روز modifies نبرد (an attributive genitive-like relation: 'the day of battle'); there is again no copula—the sense is assertive listing (these things are here). Word order is typical: adjective/phrase after the noun (مردان) and the noun phrase روز نبرد qualifies which men are meant.
segments.s305-g91: ترا here is split into the poetic enclitic ت + را: ت is the 2nd‑person object-clitic ('to you'), and را functions as the object/ethical marker. گاه ('a time/occasion') is the subject of گذشت ('passed'); گرمی و خوشی are coordinated nouns describing that time. گذشت is a past-tense verbal form meaning 'passed' or 'has passed'.
segments.s305-g92: گل و لاله و رنگ و وشی is an asyndetic list (flowers, tulips, colour and beauty/appearance) followed by the same past verb گذشت ('passed'). وشی is an older/poetic noun meaning 'manner/quality' (here: beauty/appearance). The verb is repeated at the end for the whole coordinated list, a common poetic device.
segments.s305-g93: زمستان و سرما is a coordinated subject ('winter and cold'). به پیش اندرست is a poetic phrase meaning 'is before/at hand'—به پیش ('toward/forward') plus اندرست (archaic/poetic copular construction 'is within/among' → here 'lies ahead'). The verb is archaic/poetic (اندرست) rather than the modern است.
segments.s305-g94: که introduces a consequential/clausal phrase ('so that' / 'such that'). بر نیزه‌ها ('on the spears') is a prepositional phrase (بر + plural نیزه‌ها). گردد is the present/subjunctive/epistolary form of گشت/گردیدن used here in an impersonal/resultative sense 'becomes' or 'comes to be'. افسرده دست is inverted: افسرده (adjective 'dejected/withering') modifies دست ('hand'); Persian poetry freely inverts adjective–noun order and places the predicate-adjective after گردد.
segments.s305-g95: به دامن چو ابر اندرافگند چین is a compact poetic clause. به دامن ('into the hem/skirts' — here figuratively the foothills or skirt of the land) is a locative; چو ابر ('like clouds') is a simile. اندرافگند is an older/compound verb meaning 'cast into' or 'deposited into' (poetic perfective), and چین ('folds') is its object. Word order places the locative and simile before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s305-g96: بر و بوم ما سنگ گردد زمین is a switch of elements: بر و بوم ما ('on our homeland/our dwelling') is the locative/topic, سنگ گردد ('becomes stone') is the verbal predicate where گردد is the present/future/resultative verb 'becomes', and زمین ('the ground/land') here restates the affected element or functions as a concluding emphatic noun. The clause says the homeland is turned into stone—an outcome clause with poetic inversion.
segments.s305-g41: "ز راه پدر شاه تا کیقباد" is a genitive/phrase chain meaning 'from the king's paternal line as far as Kay Qobad.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; راه پدر شاه is a construct (راه + پدر + شاه) meaning 'the king's father's way/line' (i.e. paternal line); تا introduces the limit 'as far as' and attaches to the name کیقباد.
segments.s305-g42: "ز مادر سوی تور دارد نژاد" = 'from (his) mother, toward Tur he has a lineage.' ز مادر = 'from the mother'; سوی تور is a directional phrase 'toward Tur'; دارد نژاد is a verbal phrase 'has/possesses lineage' (دارد here = present 'has'), with the noun نژاد as its object/predicate.
segments.s305-g43: "ز شاهان گیتی سرش برترست" = 'Among the kings of the world his head is higher.' ز introduces the group ('among/from the kings of the world'); شاهان گیتی is a genitive phrase 'kings of the world'; سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش = his); برترست is an archaic fused predicative form meaning 'is superior/higher' (برتر + ‑ست = 'is superior').
segments.s305-g44: "به چین نام او تخت را افسرست" is a poetic clause: به چین ('in the array/formation' or 'by the folds/array of') qualifies نام او ('his name/reputation'); تخت را is the definite object 'the throne'; افسرست is a predicative formation meaning 'is a crest/ornament' (افسر = crest/helmet, ‑ست = 'is'). Together: 'by the array of his fame, his name is a crest for the throne.'
segments.s305-g45: "به ابر اندرون تیز پران عقاب" is a compact image: به ابر اندرون = 'into/within the cloud'; تیز پران is a compound adjective 'sharp/fast‑winged' or 'swift‑winged' modifying عقاب (eagle). Word order is poetic (locative phrase then adjectives then noun). There is no finite verb — a nominal/clausal image.
segments.s305-g46: "نهنگ دلاور به دریای آب" = 'a valiant whale to/in the sea of water.' نهنگ دلاور is noun + epithet 'valiant whale'; به + دریای آب is a locative/directional phrase 'to/in the sea (of) water' where دریای is the construct form of دریا + ی (sea of) linking to آب.
segments.s305-g47: "همه پاسبانان تخت ویند" is a nominal clause: همه ('all') + پاسبانان تخت ('guards of the throne') with ویند, an archaic plural copular/ending (‑vind), giving 'are' — so: 'All are the guards of the throne.' ویند functions as the plural copula.
segments.s305-g48: "دد و دام شادان به بخت ویند" is a parallel nominal clause: دد و دام ('foes and snares') is the subject; شادان is a predicative adjective 'joyful, rejoicing'; به بخت ('because of/by fortune') gives the cause; ویند again is the archaic plural copula 'are' — literally 'foes and snares are joyful because of fortune.'
segments.s305-g113: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. تنگ اندر آید is a verb phrase meaning 'comes tight/close in' (تنگ + اندر + آید = 'become tight/press in'); بد روزگار is a genitive-like juxtaposition 'bad times / ill fortune' describing the kind of time that is closing in.
segments.s305-g114: نخواهد is the negative/future/desiderative form 'will not want' (poetically placed before the possessor); دلم is 'my heart' (دل + ـم possessive suffix), so the phrase means 'my heart will not want/accept'. پند آموزگار is the object 'the teacher's advice' (پند = advice, آموزگار = teacher), with the whole clause stating that the speaker's heart will refuse such counsel.
segments.s305-g115: به فرمان یزدان is an instrumental phrase 'by the command of God' (به used as 'by'/'by means of' plus فرمان یزدان). به هنگام خواب is a temporal phrase 'at the time of sleep' (به هنگام = 'at the time of', خواب = 'sleep').
segments.s305-g116: شوم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative 'I may become' / 'I become'. چون = 'like/as', introducing the simile; ستاره 'star' is the likeness, and بر آفتاب indicates the relation to the sun ('in front of/relative to the sun'—poetic spatial relation).
segments.s305-g117: به دریای کیماک is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the sea of cream' (figurative rich sea); بر بگذرم is a verb 'I shall pass over/cross' where بر adds the sense 'over' and بگذرم is 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'I pass / I will pass'.
segments.s305-g118: سپارم is 'I entrust / I leave (in someone's care)'. ترا is addressed object 'to you' (here shown as the 2nd-person element ت + object marker را), so the clause means 'I entrust to you my army and kingdom' (لشکر و کشورم).
segments.s305-g119: مرا ... آرامگاه باشد has a jussive/wish sense: 'may my tomb be ...' (مرا + را form of the object/possessive is split in the word list). گنگ و دژ means 'mute (secret) and fortress'—i.e., a silent/hidden stronghold—and باشد is the optative/subjunctive 'may it be'.
segments.s305-g120: نبیند is a jussive/negative-form of دیدن used here as a wish 'may ... not see'; مرا (represented as separate elements in the list) is the object 'me'. نیز شاه و سپاه lists 'also/likewise king and army'—with the effect 'may king and army also not see me' (i.e., neither king nor troops behold me).
segments.s305-g81: نگه کن is an imperative phrase: نگه (look/watch) + کن (do!, imperative auxiliary) = 'look/heed!'. بدین is an older spelling/functional grouping for بهِ این ('with this' / 'to this'); here it attaches to گردش روزگار to mean 'with this turning of the world' (the element ب is the prepositional prefix). گردش روزگار is a genitive/nominal phrase 'the turning/circuit of fate/time' (گردش = turn/circuit; روزگار = fate/time).
segments.s305-g82: جز او را مکن is a negative imperative: جز 'except', او 'him', را is the topical/definite marker attached to او, and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not make/do not do'. بر دل آموزگار is a prepositional phrase where بر دل = 'upon (your) heart' and آموزگار = 'teacher/guide'; together the clause means 'do not make anyone but him the teacher of your heart.'
segments.s305-g83: که introduces an explanatory/casual clause ('for/because'); ما در حصاریم is a present-state clause 'we are in the fortress' (حصاریم = in/under siege or in a fortress). و links the next clause: هامون تراست is a verbless statement with تراست (poetic form of 'is yours') as copula, 'the plain (هامون) is yours.'
segments.s305-g84: This line strings two nominal phrases that share the final copula تراست: سری پر ز کین = 'a head full of vengeance' uses poetic z (ز) 'from/of' to mark the complement (پر = 'full (of)'). دل پر از خون = 'a heart full of blood' uses the more usual پر از. The final تراست applies to both phrases: 'is yours.'
segments.s305-g85: همی is an adverb meaning 'still/ever' or continuous aspect marker. گنگ خوانم is 'I call/name (it) mute': خوانم is first-person present of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/name'. بهشت منست is an equational phrase where منست (archaic/poetic form related to منس) functions as a predicate 'is fitting/is (a) paradise,' so the whole clause reads 'I still call it a mute paradise / it is (called) a mute paradise.'
segments.s305-g86: برآورده is a past-participle/adjectival participle meaning 'fulfilled/restored.' بوم و کشت منست is another equational phrase: 'the homeland (بوم) and the sowing/crops (کشت) are (now) as they should be' with منست as the poetic predicate 'is/are fitting' or 'are restored/fulfilled.' The segment therefore asserts that the speaker's land and crops have been brought to fruition.
segments.s305-g87: هم ایدر مرا گنج is an existential/possessive locative: ایدر = 'here', مرا = 'for me' (possessive/dative sense, split in the text as م + را), and the auxiliary/verb م (from هست) expresses existence ('there is / is for me'). The sequence thus means 'here is treasure for me' or 'I have treasure here.' The following و ایدر سپاه repeats the locative 'and here (is) an army.'
segments.s305-g88: This is simple enumeration with repeated locative emphasis: هم ایدر نگین = 'here, too, a gem' and هم ایدر کلاه = 'and here, too, a helmet.' هم + ایدر marks 'also/here as well' and the nouns (نگین, کلاه) are direct items present in that place.
segments.s305-g73: یکی modifies منزل as an indefinite numeral (‘not one / not a single’); اندر بیابان is a locative phrase ‘in the wilderness’; نماند is the past/Perfect sense of ماندن used here negatively to mean ‘did not remain’ (subject is implicit). Word order is inverted for poetic effect (numeral + noun + locative + verb).
segments.s305-g74: به کشور gives the location ‘in/within the realm’; جز از introduces the exception ‘except for’; دشت ویران is a noun + adjective phrase ‘desolate plain(s)’; نماند is the same past-negative verb applying to the whole clause, so: ‘in the land nothing remained except the desolate plain(s)’. The order (prepositional phrase before exception) is poetic.
segments.s305-g75: جز از introduces what is left as an exception: ‘except for’; کینه ‘hatred’ and زخم شمشیر تیز form a coordinated noun phrase where زخم (‘wound’) is qualified by the genitive/attribution ‘شمشیر تیز’ (‘the sharp sword’). The sequence lists what alone survives — hatred and sword-wounds.
segments.s305-g76: نماند is the past-negative ‘did not remain’; ز is the poetic variant of از (‘of/from’) and here marks the possessive relation ‘of us’ (ز ما = ‘from us’ or ‘of us’); نام is the head noun ‘name/reputation’; تا رستخیز is a temporal clause ‘until the Resurrection’. The clause means ‘no name of ours remained until Resurrection.’
segments.s305-g77: نیاید here functions impersonally/negatively as ‘it does not please’ or ‘would not be pleasing’; جهان‌آفرین را uses را to mark the experiencer/beneficiary (‘to the world‑creator’); پسند is a noun (or verbal-noun) ‘approval/pleasing’ so the whole phrase reads ‘it would not please the Creator.’ This is a classical, somewhat idiomatic construction.
segments.s305-g78: به فرجام پیچان شویم is a subordinate clause with شویم (present/subjunctive first-person plural of شدن) meaning ‘that we become’ or ‘that we be led to’; فرجام پیچان = ‘a twisted/crooked end’ (فرجام = outcome/end, پیچان = twisting/twisted); از گزند is the source/cause (‘by/from harm’). Together: ‘that we become (led) to a twisted end from harm.’
segments.s305-g79: وگر = ‘if’; جنگ جویی is a compound where جنگ ‘war’ and جویی (from جستن/جوییدن) express ‘seeking war’ or ‘a seeker of battle’; همی = an adverbial particle meaning ‘always’/‘continually’; بیگمان here is a vocative honorific (‘O bey/chieftain’ / ‘O noble one’) addressing the addressee. The clause is conditional and addresses someone directly.
segments.s305-g80: نیاساید is a negative-present form meaning ‘does not rest / will not be at rest’; از marks the source/reason ‘from’; کین دلت is a genitive construction where کین ‘hatred/vengeance’ belongs to دل‑ت (‘your heart’ — دل + second-person suffix ت); یک زمان is an adverbial phrase ‘for one moment / for a single time’ used here to mean ‘not even for a moment.’ The verb refers to the heart’s unrest caused by its grudge.
segments.s305-g121: چو آید ... خواستن is a temporal clause: چو = 'when'; آید is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of آمدن 'to come' meaning 'comes' or 'arrives'; مرا (here split in the data as م + را) marks the experiencer/beneficiary 'to me' (م = clitic pronoun, را = object/emphasis marker attached). روز کین خواستن is a nominalized phrase 'the day of (the) wanting/asking of vengeance' (کین + خواستن functioning as a deverbal noun phrase). The whole segment sets the time/occasion for what follows.
segments.s305-g122: ببین is an imperative or exhortative 'look/see' (addressing the addressee). آن زمان = 'that time' / 'then' (temporal demonstrative + noun). لشکر آراستن is an infinitival/participial idea 'arraying/arming the army' (آراستن as the verbal notion 'to array/arraying'); the verb is implied as the event to be observed. Word order is typical Persian (verb phrase at end for the action being observed).
segments.s305-g123: بیایم بخواهم is a purposive/intent construction: بیایم is 1st-person-form (optative/subjunctive flavor) 'let me come / I shall come', and بخواهم is 1st-person present/subjunctive of خواستن 'to ask/want' meaning 'to demand/ask'; ز تو = 'from you'; کین خویش = 'his/one's own vengeance' (خوی + enclitic ش = possessive). The clause expresses purposive motion — the speaker will come in order to demand vengeance from the addressee.
segments.s305-g124: به هر جای = 'to/at every place' (به as preposition 'to/at', هر جای 'whichever/any place'); پیدا کنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive or habitual 'I find / I may find' (پیدا used adjectivally/verbal with کنم 'I make/find'); دین خویش = 'my/one's due/claim' (دین here = debt/obligation/right). Together the segment says the speaker will recover/locate his due everywhere he goes.
segments.s305-g125: و‌گر = 'and if' (conditional marker). کینه = 'hatred/grudge' (direct object of the action). از مغز بیرون = 'out of (the) mind/brain' (از = from; مغز = mind/brain; بیرون = out). کنی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن 'to do/make' and here means 'you remove/expel' (make go out). The clause is a protasis (if-clause) describing a hypothetical action by the addressee.
segments.s305-g126: به مهر = 'with love/affection' (به indicating manner or instrument, مهر = love/affection). اندر این کشور = 'within this country/realm' (اندر = in/within; این کشور = this land). افسون کنی = 'you enchant/charm' (افسون as noun 'charm' with کنی 'you make/do' = you enchant). Together this is the apodosis: if hatred is expelled, you will fill/enchant the land with love.
segments.s305-g127: گشایم is 1st-person singular subjunctive/future-tinged verb from گشودن 'to open/reveal' meaning 'I will open / I shall lay open'; در گنج = 'in the treasure(hoard)' (در = in; گنج = treasure/treasury). تاج و کمر = 'crown and belt/girdle' (listing valuables). The verb announces the speaker's action of revealing or distributing treasures.
segments.s305-g128: همان = 'that very / the same' (emphatic demonstrative); تخت و دینار و جام گهر is a parallel list of regal treasures: تخت = throne, دینار = dinar/coin (money), جام = cup, گهر = gem/jewel. The sequence names the specific valuables the speaker will reveal or distribute.
segments.s305-g65: 'نگه کن' is an imperative compound (نگه + کن) meaning 'look' or 'behold'. The conjunction/particle 'که' introduces the subordinate/exclamatory clause ('that' → here functioning like '— how'), and 'تا چند' together functions as an exclamative 'how many' (در کلاسیک فارسی 'تا' can intensify 'چند'). 'شهر فراخ' is a noun + adjective phrase ('city' + 'spacious'); the adjective فراخ is postposed here for poetic rhythm.
segments.s305-g66: پر از is a fixed adjectival phrase meaning 'full of'. The preposition/particle 'از' here marks the contents ('full of X' = 'پر از X'). The list باغ و ایوان و میدان و کاخ is a simple coordintated noun series describing what the cities are full of; the coordinating و is repeated for rhetorical cadence.
segments.s305-g67: شدست (shodast) is a literary past-form meaning 'it became' or 'there came to be'. 'اندر این کینه' is a locative/causal prepositional phrase 'in/within this grudge', and 'جستن خراب' is a verbal-nominal construction 'seeking ruin' (جستن 'to seek' with خراب functioning as 'ruin/destruction' here). Together the clause says that from this grudge an attempt to bring ruin arose.
segments.s305-g68: بهانه is the head noun 'pretext' or 'excuse' and the two following proper names (سیاووش و افراسیاب) stand in apposition, linked by و. Grammatically this names the pretext: 'the pretext: Siyâvash and Afrâsiâb.'
segments.s305-g69: همان modifies کارزاری to mean 'that same' or 'the same' campaign; کارزاری is an adjectival/attributive form meaning 'of the campaign' or 'campaign-related'; سواران (subject) follows and is the plural noun 'horsemen/ mounted warriors'; جنگ here is a postposed noun qualifying the activity/context ('in battle' or 'of battle'), and the whole phrase lacks an explicit verb because Persian often stacks noun phrases before the predicate in the next segment.
segments.s305-g70: This segment contains two parallel adverbial similes: 'به تن همچو پیل' = 'in (their) body like an elephant' and 'به زور نهنگ' = 'in (their) might/strength like a whale.' به is used adverbially ('in/by ...-ness') to mark the domain (body, strength); تن and زور are the nouns being compared; همچو introduces the simile; و links the two comparisons. The clause finishes the description begun in the previous segment (the implied predicate is something like 'were').
segments.s305-g71: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that/for whom'); جز before a noun phrase means 'except' or 'other than'; کام شیران is a genitival phrase 'the desire/appetite of the lions' (کام = desire/appetite, شیران = lions), and کفنشان ('their shroud') is the possessed noun (کفن + the pronoun شان). The clause ends with نبود, the past negative copula 'there was not / they had not,' so the structure reads 'there was no shroud except the lions' appetite.'
segments.s305-g72: سری here means 'a head' (not 'secret'); نیز functions as 'also/even' (giving the sense 'not even'); نزدیک is the adjective 'near' governing the following possesive phrase تنشان ('their bodies'); نبود is again the past negative copula. Word order is Subject (سری) — adjunct (نیز) — predicate (نزدیک تنشان نبود), yielding 'not even a head was near their bodies.'
segments.s305-g105: "به شمشیر بگذارم این انجمن" uses the idiom 'به ... گذاردن'—here 'بگذارم' (1st‑person present/subjunctive) means 'I will put/put to' with 'به شمشیر' indicating the instrument or fate ('to the sword'). The direct object 'این انجمن' follows the verb without an object marker because this is elevated/poetic word order.
segments.s305-g106: Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: 'به دست تو آیم' literally 'I come into your hand(s)' (آیم = I come/fall), and 'گرفتار من' is a predicative adjective phrase 'I, captured.' The verb آیم is first‑person present/subjunctive/optative used here as a future/inevitable action 'I shall fall.'
segments.s305-g107: مپندار is the negative imperative 'do not suppose/think'; it introduces the subordinate clause 'کاین نیز نابود نیست' ('that this also is not destroyed'), where 'نابود' (predicate) precedes the copula 'نیست' in typical classical/poetic order.
segments.s305-g108: This line states a general truth. 'نساید' is a present negative verb meaning 'does not endure / is not lasting' (literally 'is worn away'); 'کسی کو' functions as 'anyone who / someone who' (کو here is a relative/archaic 'who'); 'نفرسود نیست' means 'is not unworn' (i.e. everyone wears out). The final 'نیست' negates existence — 'there is no one who does not wear out.'
segments.s305-g109: 'نبیره' (scion/offspring) is followed by epithets: 'سر خسروان' = 'chief/head of kings' (an honorific group‑label), and 'زادشم' is given as a proper name in apposition; overall this names a royal descendant.
segments.s305-g110: 'ز پشت فریدون' is a poetic/genitive expression 'from the back/lineage of Fereydun' ('ز' = from, 'پشت' = back/lineage). The following 'وز تخم جم' should be read as 'و ز تخم جم' ('and from the seed of Jam'); here 'وز' is a contraction of 'و ز' (and from) rather than the lexical senses listed. The phrase lists ancestral origin.
segments.s305-g111: The construction 'مرا دانش ایزدی هست و فر' uses 'مرا' ('for me' / 'I have') with 'دانش ایزدی' ('divine knowledge') as the possessed noun, 'هست' as the existential/copular verb, and 'و فر' appending 'and farr (royal glory)'. In elevated Persian 'مرا ... هست' = 'I have ...'.
segments.s305-g112: 'همان' is a demonstrative 'that very / the same'; 'یاورم' = 'my helper/ally' (یاور + possessive ـم). 'ایزد دادگر' is an appositive noun phrase 'the just God' (ایزد = deity, دادگر = just), serving as the nominative/predicative complement: 'That same just God is my helper.'
segments.s305-g1: 'دگر روز' = 'the next/other day' (adverbial time phrase). 'چون' is a subordinating conjunction here ('as/when'). 'خور' is the noun 'sun' and 'برآمد' is the simple past 'arose' (3rd sg.). 'ز راغ' is a prepositional phrase 'from/of longing' (ز = poetic 'from', راغ = 'longing'), giving a poetical cause/manner: 'the sun rose from longing.'
segments.s305-g2: 'نهاد' is the past of نهادن here ('placed/put', 3rd sg.). The phrase 'از بر' is an older/pre-classical combination with a locative/relative sense ('on/upon' or 'from upon'); 'چرخ زرین' = 'golden wheel' (چرخ = sky/heaven, modified by زرین). The direct object 'چراغ' ('lamp') comes at the end: literally 'placed a lamp upon the golden wheel of the sky.'
segments.s305-g3: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/uproar'. 'برآمد' (past) 'arose' is the verb. 'بلند' here qualifies the sound ('loud/high') and is positioned after the verb for poetic word order. 'از حصار' is a prepositional phrase 'from the fortress' showing origin.
segments.s305-g4: 'پر اندیشه شد' is a predicate: 'پر' = 'full (of)', 'اندیشه' = 'thought', 'شد' = 'became' (past of شدن used as copula/resultive). 'زان' = 'from/for that' (introducing cause: 'because of that'), and 'سخن شهریار' = 'the speech/words of the king' (older Persian omits the ezāfe; noun + possessor follow without a linker).
segments.s305-g5: 'همانگه' is an adverb 'at that moment/instantly'. Here 'در' functions as 'gate(s)/door(s)' (older usage allows در = gate) and is the subject that 'گشادند' (past, 3rd pl.) agrees with: 'the gates opened.' 'باز' is an adverb here meaning 'again' or 'opened' in poetic usage; overall: 'the gates of the fortress opened again.'
segments.s305-g6: 'برهنه شد' = 'became bare/exposed' (برهنه adjective + شد 'became'). 'از روی پوشیده' is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from the covered surface' (از = from, روی = 'face/surface'), with 'پوشیده' = 'hidden/covered' modifying 'راز' ('secret') that follows; classical Persian often places modifiers in this order without an ezāfe. The line means the previously hidden secret was exposed.
segments.s305-g7: 'بیامد' is the past tense 'came' (3rd sg.). 'ز دژ جهن' = 'from the fortress Jahan' (proper name follows the noun without an ezāfe in older style). 'با ده سوار' = 'with ten horsemen' (numeral + noun: classical Persian often uses the singular noun after the numeral).
segments.s305-g8: A triad of epithets joined by 'و': 'خردمند' = 'wise', 'بادانش' = 'possessing knowledge / learned' (a compound with با + دانش; a lexicalized adjective in poetry), and 'مایه‌دار' = 'wealthy' or 'well-endowed'. The adjectives qualify the (implied) rider(s)/leader who arrived.
segments.s305-g9: بشد is an older/poetic past-form of شدن used here to mean 'he went/advanced' (third person). پیش is the preposition 'in front of / before' placed before the following noun phrase. دهلیز پرده سرای is a stacked noun phrase: دهلیز 'vestibule/corridor' modified by پرده سرای 'of the curtained chamber' (classical style of linking nouns where the modifiers follow).
segments.s305-g10: همی بود is the habitual/continuous past construction: همی here is a progressive/adverbial particle ('was / there was / were present'), and بود is the past of بودن. با marks accompaniment 'with', and نامداران is the plural noun 'renowned/illustrious ones'. به پای is a locative phrase 'at the foot (of)' with به as the preposition and پای 'foot'.
segments.s305-g11: از آن پس is a temporal sequence 'after that / thereafter'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. منوشان is a proper name (Manūshān) and گرد here indicates a group/retinue or 'with his company' (classical usage where گرد follows a personal name to indicate entourage).
segments.s305-g12: This clause contains the object-marked phrase جهن را (جهن = proper name; را = definite object marker) and a verbal phrase پیش برد. خرد یافته is a compound adjective 'having acquired wisdom / the wise' modifying جهن. پیش برد here functions as a verbal complex 'led/brought forward (before someone)' — پیش is adverbial 'forward/before' and برد is the verb 'to carry/take' (classical usage meaning 'brought/led').
segments.s305-g13: خردمند is the noun/adjective 'the wise man'. چون before a verb here is a temporal conjunction 'when' (classical Persian uses چون = 'when'), and پیش خسرو 'in Khosrow's presence' places the action before the king. رسید is the past 'arrived/reached.' The clause order is subject — temporal conjunction — locative — verb.
segments.s305-g14: شد is the simple past/become auxiliary here introducing the state 'became / was' in combination with the following adjective. از is the preposition 'from/of'. آب‌دیده modifies رخش and here describes the horse as 'water‑seen / tear‑eyed' (a poetic characterization). رخش is the horse/steed, and ناپدید is the adjective 'vanished / gone' — together: 'his tear‑eyed steed became vanished' (i.e., 'the steed disappeared').
segments.s305-g15: بماند is the past 'remained' (third person). اندر او is the locative 'in/within him'. جهن is a proper name (Jahan) and جنگی شگفت is a nominal phrase where جنگی 'martial/warlike' and شگفت 'wonderful/strange' together describe a remarkable warlike feeling or impulse — classical Persian often uses such adjectival stacks to denote an internal state.
segments.s305-g16: This is a simple action clause: کلاه بزرگی 'a large helmet' (کلاه noun + بزرگی adjective/attribute), ز سر 'from the head' with z = poetic 'from', and بر گرفت is the compound verb 'بر گرفتن' = 'to take up / to take off' (here 'to take off from the head'). Persian often splits the verbal compound so that the preverb بر appears before the main verb گرفت.
segments.s305-g97: ز هر سو که خوانم بیاید سپاه is a relative/conditional clause: ز هر سو 'from every side' (ز = poetic 'from', each word in modifier order), که خوانم 'that I call/summon' uses خوانم as first-person present/subjunctive-like 'I call' (here = 'I summon'), and بیاید سپاه places the verb بیاید 'may/shall come' before the noun سپاه 'army/host' (poetic word order: verb + subject). The clause means 'from whatever side I summon, an army will come.'
segments.s305-g98: نتابی تو با گردش هور و ماه: نتابی is negative-present (ن + تابى) addressing 'you' — here 'you do not/cannot endure/last' (تابیدن can mean 'to endure' in older/poetic usage); تو is the explicit subject, and با گردش هور و ماه is an instrumental/contrast phrase 'with the turning/revolution of sun and moon' (گردش = rotation/cycle, هور و ماه = sun and moon). The clause says you will not withstand the cycles of sun and moon.
segments.s305-g99: ور ایدون گمانی که هر کارزار groups an if/hypothesis: ور = 'if', ایدون (here an adverb/particle in older poetic usage) marks 'now/at this point' or emphasis on the supposition, and گمانی 'a supposition/imagining' is the noun governed by ور; که هر کارزار 'that in every battle' begins the content clause (که = 'that').
segments.s305-g100: ترا بردهد اختر روزگار is the result clause: ترا is the 2nd-person object (here split orthographically into ت + را), بردهد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive-like verb 'may give/bestow' (poetic inflection), and اختر روزگار is a compound 'star of fate/time' (اختر = star, روزگار = fate/time). Word order is object–verb–subject (poetic), so 'the star of fate bestows upon you.'
segments.s305-g101: از اندیشه گردون مگر بگذرد: از اندیشه گردون is a genitive-like phrase 'from the thought/design of the heavens' (اندیشه = thought/design, گردون = the sky/heaven or fate), مگر functions here as 'unless' (classical/poetic use), and بگذرد is 3rd-person subjunctive/present 'passes'/'lets pass'. Together: 'unless the heavens' purpose/plan passes (i.e. unless fate allows otherwise)'.
segments.s305-g102: ز رنج تو دیگر کسی برخورد: ز رنج تو = 'from/at your suffering' (z = from, رنج = suffering, تو = genitive/possessive 'your'), دیگر کسی = 'no other person / no one else' (دیگر used as 'other/no other'), and برخورد is a nominal/verb 'encounter/meet' here meaning 'will encounter' or 'will come across'. The clause therefore states that, unless fate changes, no one else will meet/escape your suffering (context determines whether it's 'no one else will be spared' or 'no one else will encounter you').
segments.s305-g103: گر ایدونک گویی که ترکان چین: گر = 'if'; ایدونک is an enclitic/particle (poetic) adding immediacy or hesitation ('even now'/'at this moment'); گویی here functions syntactically as 'you say' (classical/poetic 2nd-person present), though the provided lemma senses are nominal — the verb sense 'you say' is the operative reading; که introduces the content clause 'that the Turks (ترکان) [take] China (چین)'.
segments.s305-g104: بگیرم زنم آسمان بر زمین: a two-verb sequence in first-person singular: بگیرم 'I seize/take hold of' (present/subjunctive-like or future-oriented in poetry) and زنم 'I strike' (I will smite), followed by the object phrase آسمان بر زمین 'the sky upon the earth' meaning 'to strike/bring the sky down upon the earth' (hyperbolic poetic image). Word order places verbs before the direct image for forceful effect.
segments.s305-g17: چو آمد is a temporal clause: چو (a subordinating particle 'when') + آمد (past tense 'came'), so 'when he came'. به نزدیک تختش is a prepositional phrase 'near/close to his throne' where تختش is 'his throne' (takhteš = تخت + possessive -ش). فراز postposes and qualifies تخت (a poetic adjective/noun 'on high, lofty'), giving 'his lofty throne'.
segments.s305-g18: بر او آفرین کرد is literal 'he made/uttered (آفرین) upon/to him' — آفرین functions as an acclamation/noun ('praise, “afreen”') and کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made' used here as 'uttered/praised'. و links the two actions. بردش نماز is an idiomatic past construction: برد (past of بردن 'to take/carry') + -ش (3rd‑person object 'him') + نماز (object 'prayer'), so 'and (he) took him to prayer.'
segments.s305-g19: چنین گفت = 'thus said' with چنین ('thus') modifying the verb گفت (past 'said'); note the supplied senses list treats گفت as a noun, but here it functions as the verb 'he said'. کای is a vocative/title (ʻO, Kayʼ) addressed to the king; نامور شهریار are attributive epithets 'renowned sovereign/king'.
segments.s305-g20: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. جهان را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the world' as direct object/topic). به شادی گذار contains به شادی ('in/with joy') and گذار, here a jussive/imperative-form meaning 'let/pass/spend' (poetic use: 'may you pass/spend'), so the clause is an optative wish 'may you always pass the world in joy.'
segments.s305-g21: بر و بوم ما is a fixed pair meaning 'our land and home' (بر here functions as part of the compound 'بر و بوم', archaic for 'land/soil' with ما 'our'). بر تو فرخنده باد is an optative clause: بر (upon/for) تو (you) فرخنده ('auspicious') + باد (optative particle 'may it be'), i.e. 'may our land and home be auspicious for you.'
segments.s305-g22: دل و چشم بدخواه تو is a coordinated object 'the heart and the eye of your ill-wisher' (بدخواه تو = 'your ill-wisher/enemy'). کنده باد is again optative: کنده ('uprooted/removed') + باد ('may it be'), i.e. 'may [their] heart and eye be uprooted.' The optative particle باد at the end governs the whole clause.
segments.s305-g23: همیشه is 'always'. The phrase بدی شاد is elliptical/poetic — in context it expresses a wish that goodness or 'well-being' be glad/happy (بدی here is ambiguous in the supplied sense list but functions positively in context). و یزدان‌پرست links 'the pious' (یزدان‌پرست = 'God‑worshipper, pious person'). Overall the segment offers two coordinated desiderata: 'may goodness be jubilant and the pious...' (poetic economy of expression).
segments.s305-g24: بر و بوم ما repeats 'our land and home' as beneficiary/scene. پیش گسترده دست is an idiom: پیش ('before/in the presence of') + گسترده ('spread out/extended') + دست ('hand(s)'). Together it means 'may [they] extend/spread out their hands before our land' — i.e. may pious people offer help/support to our land. Note word order is poetic (object/beneficiary fronted).
segments.s305-g57: جگر خسته‌ام is a verbless clause with a nominal subject جگر (‘heart’ in a figurative/seat‑of‑feeling sense) followed by the predicate خسته‌ام (خسته + the 1st‑person enclitic -م = 'I am tired'). ز این سخن is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of/from these words', and پر ز درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of pain/sorrow' (پر + ز + noun). The elements are arranged in poetic order but mean 'my heart is tired because of these words and full of sorrow.'
segments.s305-g58: نشسته is a participial/adjectival predicate 'sitting/lying, settled'; به یکسو is a prepositional phrase 'to/at one side.' ز خواب و ز خورد uses the ablative preposition ز twice ('from/through/owing to sleep and to eating'); خورد here stands as a nominalized verbal stem meaning 'eating/food.' The phrase describes the state of being collapsed to one side from sleep and from eating.
segments.s305-g59: نه negates the clause that follows: نه من کشتم او را = 'It was not I who killed him.' کشتم is the past tense verb 'I killed' with the 1st‑person ending -م (here the lexical senses list did not include the verbal sense, so the appropriate sense index is not available). او را uses را as the definite/object marker. که introduces the ensuing explanatory/subordinate clause 'that ...'. ناپاک دیو is a noun phrase 'an unclean/impure demon' that functions as the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s305-g60: ببرد is a form of بردن (here 'take/carry away'); in this clause it conveys 'carried off / took away.' از دلم is the ablative 'from my heart' (دلم = دل + suffix 'my' — the provided separate token م does not match the listed sense and is treated as non‑matching). ترس گیهان is 'the fear of the world' (گیهان = the world), and خدیو is an epithet/appositive 'the ruler/khedive' qualifying the worldly power. Grammatically the verb takes the subject (ناپاک دیو) and the object expressed by the ablative phrase 'from my heart'.
segments.s305-g61: زمانه here means 'fate/fortune' (agentive sense). ورا is 'for/to him' (vora = به + را in older usage), بد بهانه is 'a bad pretext/excuse.' مرا = me (object) is split in the tokenization (the separate token m does not match the provided '#هست' sense), and را is the object marker. The line says essentially 'Fate gave him a bad pretext (against me).' Note the poetic order and the use of ورا to indicate the beneficiary/target.
segments.s305-g62: به چنگ اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into the grasp/clutch within' (چنگ 'claw, clutch' used figuratively as 'snare'), بد فسانه is 'evil/wicked tales,' and again م + را mark the object 'me' (me was cast into that snare). The verb implied from the previous clause governs this result (i.e. fate 'threw/placed' me into this inner grasp of bad tales).
segments.s305-g63: تو اکنون خردمندی و پادشا is a verbless equational statement: 'You are now wise and (a) king.' Persian often omits a copula ('are') in such nominal predicates; خردمندی ('wisdom' or 'being wise') and پادشا ('king') are predicative nouns linked by و 'and.' The order places the person (تو) and time modifier (اکنون) up front for emphasis.
segments.s305-g64: پذیرنده مردم پارسا is a noun phrase appositive/predicate: پذیرنده is a present participle 'one who receives/accepts' (here 'patron' or 'welcoming one'), followed by its object مردم پارسا 'pious people' (مردم 'people' qualified by پارسا 'pious'). The phrase characterizes the king as a host/patron of the pious.
segments.s305-g137: همه لشکرت را توانگر کنم is structured with the object phrase (همه لشکرت را = 'your whole army' + ra as definite/object marker) before the verb توانگر کنم. کنَم is the classical 1st-person present/subjunctive (here volitional: 'I will make'), with the subject omitted (I). Note that لشکرت contains the 2nd-person possessive -ت ('your').
segments.s305-g138: ترا تخت زرین و افسر کنم places the object pronoun (ترا = تو + را, i.e. 'you' with the object marker) before the items given as what will be made/given (تخت زرین و افسر = 'a golden throne and a helmet'); کنم is again 1st-person volitional 'I will make/give'. افسر here is an archaic/poetic word for a helmet/crown-like headpiece.
segments.s305-g139: همت یار باشم به هر کارزار literally places the noun همت ('resolve, zeal') followed by یار ('companion') and the verb باشم ('may I be' / optative 1st-person), so the speaker expresses a wish: 'may my resolve be your companion.' به هر کارزار is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in every battle.'
segments.s305-g140: به هر انجمن خوانمت شهریار: به هر انجمن ('in every assembly') is an adverbial phrase; خوانمت is a 1st-person verb with the enclitic object -ت ('I call/address you'), here meaning 'I will proclaim/call you'; شهریار is the predicate noun 'king.' The verb carries the speaker's declarative/performative sense.
segments.s305-g141: گر از پند من سر بپیچی همی is a conditional clause: گر = 'if'; از پند من = 'from my advice' (i.e. 'my counsel'); سر بپیچی is a common idiom with سر ('head') + بپیچی ('you turn') meaning 'you turn away' or 'you disregard'; همی is the old continuous/emphatic particle marking habitual or general condition. The clause is prototypically conditional and open-ended.
segments.s305-g142: و‌گر با نیاکان بسیچی همی continues the conditional with وگر ('and if'); با نیاکان = 'with (your) ancestors'; بسیچی is used here in the sense of a fighter/warrior (an old or dialectal word for one who fights), and همی again gives the general/habitual flavor. Grammatically it's a second conditional alternative parallel to the previous clause.
segments.s305-g143: چو ز این باز گردی بیارای جنگ begins with چو ('when/as'), followed by a prepositional phrase ز این ('from this') and باز گردی, where باز = 'again' and گردی is the 2nd-person present of گردیدن ('you return/turn'). بیارای جنگ is an imperative or exhortation 'array/prepare the war' (verb + object). The line as a whole gives a temporal clause ('when you return...') followed by the command to prepare for battle.
segments.s305-g144: منم ساخته جنگ را چون پلنگ puts the speaker forward with منم ('I too' / topical 'I') followed by ساخته (a participial or perfect form 'having made/prepared') and جنگ را where را marks the definite object. چون پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard.' Together it means the speaker is also prepared for battle, compared to a leopard in ferocity.
segments.s305-g33: چنین گفت is a fixed sequence meaning “thus said” (چنین = 'thus/so' placed before the verb). گفت is the 3rd-person past of گفتن (here ‘said’); the agent کافراسیاب follows the verb (typical older/epic word order: verb before subject). با شاه uses با as a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/with the king' (here: 'said to the king').
segments.s305-g34: نشستست is an archaic/classical 3rd-person form meaning 'he sat/is seated' (the -ست ending is characteristic of epic usage). پر before a noun means 'full (of)' (poetic adjective placed before the noun): پر درد = 'full of sorrow/pain', and مژگان پر آب is a parallel adjective phrase 'eyelashes full of water' (i.e. teary-eyed). The conjunction و links the two descriptive phrases.
segments.s305-g35: نخستین ('first') qualifies درودی ('a greeting'); the noun phrase (نخستین درودی) is followed by رسانم, a 1st-person form with -م indicating the speaker's action ('I convey/send'). به شاه is the indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to the king.' In Ferdowsi's diction رسانم functions as a narrative past/action by the speaker.
segments.s305-g36: از introduces source or cause here ('because of'/'from'); the following nouns form a possessive/genitive chain typical of classical Persian (داغِ دِلِ شاهِ توران = 'the burning grief of the king of Turan'). سپاه (army/host) stands with that chain as the referent tied into the next clause. Note that poetic word-order omits some linking particles (ezāfe) that modern prose would make explicit.
segments.s305-g37: که at the start links this clause as an explanation/cause. یزدان سپاس is a verbless exclamation 'thanks to God' (سپاس is used nominally as 'thanks/praise'). و connects the exclamation to بدویم پناه, where بدویم (from دویدن) means 'I run/hasten/flee' (1st-person form) and پناه is the noun 'refuge' — the object/goal (i.e. 'I run to Him as refuge'), with the object (God) implied from the previous phrase.
segments.s305-g38: که again introduces a subordinate clause. فرزند is the direct object 'a child/son' governed by the verb دیدم (first-person past 'I saw' / 'I beheld'). ب + دین here function together as the archaic/prepositional compound بدین ('in/at this'); پایگاه is the location 'stronghold/base.' In poetry the components may be split; read بدین پایگاه = 'in/at this stronghold.'
segments.s305-g39: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. لشکر کشد uses کشد (3rd-person form of کشیدن) in the sense 'to raise/march/lead (an army)' — an epic usage; the verb precedes the subject phrase as is common in older style. شهریاری کند pairs the noun شهریاری ('sovereignty/rule') with the light verb کند ('does/makes') to mean 'to assume sovereignty' or 'to become king.' The sequence expresses actions the referent will take.
segments.s305-g40: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of/before'; سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders.' سواری کند is the verbal expression where سواری (riding/mounted force) plus کند ('does') yields 'to ride' or 'to lead mounted troops' — here meaning 'to ride before/in front of the horsemen.' The final کند is the 3rd-person verb form completing the action.
segments.s305-g129: که here is a relative marker 'that/whom' introducing a relative clause; the clause reads literally 'that Tūr Fereydun to Irāj did not give' (word order subject–object–verb). نداد is the past simple negative of دادن ('did not give'), so the whole segment means 'whom Fereydun did not give to Irāj.'
segments.s305-g130: تو بردار is an imperative: تو (you) + بردار (take, lift). وز here is a poetic/archaic prepositional or adverbial element (used in older Persian instead of/from or with the sense of a burden), followed by کین (hatred/vengeance); مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do', and هیچ یاد = 'any remembrance/trace' (هیچ functioning as 'any/at all'). The sequence therefore commands: 'You take (him), and do not (make/keep) any remembrance from/for hatred.'
segments.s305-g131: و‌گر is the conditional 'and if'; چین و ماچین is a fixed poetic pair (here referring to people/maids associated with China or exotic regions) and functions as the object of بگیری, the present/subjunctive form of گرفتن used here like an imperatival/optative 'you take'. رواست is composed of the adjective روا 'permissible/fitting' plus the copula ست 'is', so 'it is permitted.'
segments.s305-g132: بدان is the imperative 'know (this)' formed from ب + دان (the verb 'know' / 'consider'); رای = 'counsel/plan/decision.' The following words form a compact, poetic clause: ران here is an older/poetic element tied to motion or driving (the exact idiomatic role is compacted in verse), دل is 'heart', همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle, and هواست = هوا + ست 'is desire/longing.' Overall this segment means 'Know that by that counsel (or for that reason) the heart is moved/driven by desire.' Note: some words (ران, کت) are archaisms/poetic contractions and the line shows compressed word-order typical of epic verse.
segments.s305-g133: This is a coordinator list: خراسان و مکران زمین = 'Khorasan and Makran and the land/earth'. پیش تست here is an older, idiomatic phrase meaning 'are before you / at your disposal' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', تست functioning as the copular predicate in this epic diction). Word order places the possessions before the predicate, typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s305-g134: مرا شادکامی = 'my happiness' with مرا marking possession/endowment ('for me'). کم و بیش = 'less and more' i.e. 'in small and great measure' (a fixed expression). تست here again plays the function of the copula in this poetic register ('is yours' / 'is for you' in context): the whole segment says 'my happiness, little or much, is yours.'
segments.s305-g135: به راهی 'on the road / by the route' (به + راهی). که introduces a relative clause modifying راهی: بگذشت is the past tense 'passed/went by' (subject: کاووس شاه 'King Kāvus'). So the clause reads 'on the road which King Kāvus passed.'
segments.s305-g136: فرستمت is a past-tense perfective with the enclitic object -ت ('I sent you'). چندان که باید = 'as much/so many as was necessary' (چندان = 'to that extent' + که introduces the clause 'that it is necessary'); باید here is modal 'it is necessary / ought' and سپاه is the noun 'army/troops' — the sense: 'I sent you as many troops as were required.'
segments.s305-g25: خجسته شدن is adjective + شدن ('to become') forming 'to become auspicious'; باد here is the optative particle 'may' (classical/poetic usage) placed after the verbal/nominalized phrase to express a wish. The conjunction و links this wish to the parallel infinitive باز آمدن ('to return / to come again'), so the whole segment reads as two parallel optative wishes.
segments.s305-g26: به نیکی is an adverbial phrase of manner ('in a good way' — here 'by/–ly' sense of به + the noun نیکی = 'with goodness'). همی is the archaic/prose particle marking continuous or habitual aspect ('ever, continually') and precedes the verb-like constituent داستانها زدن, where زدن is used idiomatically with داستانها to mean 'to recite/tell tales' (classical collocation). Word order is manner → adverbial particle → subject (داستانها) → verb (زدن).
segments.s305-g27: پیامی is the object 'a message' (indefinite/accusative -i). گزارم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/formal present ('I send / I will cause to be delivered') — classical Persian often uses this form for the speaker's intention. ز (poetic form of از) introduces the source 'from Afrasiab'. The whole phrase thus means 'I will/shall bring/send a message from Afrasiab.'
segments.s305-g28: اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. شاه را uses the object-marker را with شاه; in older prose this marked the person affected by the action and often appears where modern Persian would use a nominative; here it indicates 'the king' as the one affected. زان ('from/ because of that') gives the cause or reference for the following verb. نگیرد is negative present (3rd person) of گرفتن 'to take/seize' — 'does not take' — and شتاب (a noun) is the thing 'haste' that he does or does not take. Together: 'if the king does not, because of that, take to haste' (i.e. does not hurry because of it).
segments.s305-g29: چو here means 'when' (temporal). از introduces the source/agent 'from Jahan' (جهن is a proper name). گفتار is the noun 'speech/words' and بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. The subject شاه comes at the end (typical classical word order: verb before subject), so the clause reads 'when the king heard the speech from Jahan.'
segments.s305-g30: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-form verb meaning roughly 'he commanded/said (in courtly register)'. زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying یکی 'one' (a certain one / one piece). پیشگاه is an adverbial/noun meaning 'before the throne / in the presence (of the king)'. The verb governs the following noun phrase: 'he ordered (that) a golden one be brought before the court.'
segments.s305-g31: نهادند is 3rd-person plural past 'they placed / they put down'. زیر is the preposition 'under / beneath'. خردمند (adjective) precedes مرد (noun) giving 'the wise man' — adjective + noun order is normal here. Literally: 'they placed (it) under the wise man' or more idiomatically 'they set it down and the wise man (was) beneath/there.'
segments.s305-g32: نشست is past tense 'he sat' (or 'he took his seat'). و simply coordinates the clauses. پیام پدر یاد کرد is a periphrastic classical construction: یاد (a noun 'remembrance') + کرد (past of 'to do') functions together as the verb 'he remembered' with پیام پدر as its object 'his father's message'. So: 'he sat and remembered his father's message.'
segments.s305-g49: بزرگان is the head noun 'the great ones/nobles'. که introduces a relative clause describing them. با ... با repeats the preposition for emphasis ('with crown and with ornament'). زیورند is a predicative form meaning 'are adorned/are decked' (3rd-person plural); here it functions like a copular/adjectival predicate attached to بزرگان.
segments.s305-g50: به روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on the face of the earth' (به + روی + زمین). مر ترا is an older clitic sequence meaning 'in my sight / to me — you': مر = 'to me', ت = second‑person clitic attached to the following را, and را is the object/emphasis marker (here forming ترا = 'you' as the comparison standard). کهترند is the plural predicate 'are inferior/less' — the clause therefore says they are inferior to you in my judgment.
segments.s305-g51: شگفتی‌تر از is the comparative 'more astonishing than' with از marking the standard of comparison. کار دیو is a genitive (noun + possessor) 'the deed/work of the demon', and نژند is a postpositive adjective 'base/worthless' modifying کار.
segments.s305-g52: که introduces the subordinate clause explaining the wonder. هرگز is the negative adverb 'never' placed before the verbal predicate. نخواهد here functions as a future/auxiliary ('will not' / 'will (not) do'), followed by the indirect object به ما ('to/for us'). جز + noun expresses exception — جز گزند = 'except harm'. The whole clause: 'that he will never do to us anything except harm.'
segments.s305-g53: بدان is built from ب- + دان; in this context it functions like 'with regard to / given' or 'concerning that' (classical/poetic usage). مهربانی 'kindness' is the noun governed by that prepositional/introductory element. و آن راستی 'and that truth' simply conjoins another topical noun (آن = 'that', راستی = 'truth/uprightness').
segments.s305-g54: چرا = 'why' introduces the question. شد is past (become/happen) and here marks the change: 'became/has become.' دل من is the subject 'my heart', where من is the possessor. سوی + noun is a directional phrase 'toward(s)'; کاستی 'lack/deficiency' is the goal. Literally: 'Why did my heart become turned toward deficiency?'
segments.s305-g55: که introduces the clause that follows. بر دست is an agentive/ablative phrase 'by the hand of' (دست here means 'hand' / 'agency'), and من is the agent 'me' (i.e., 'by my hand'). پور is the patronymic element 'son (of)', so پور کاووس شاه reads 'son of Kavus, the king' and functions appositively to identify the person being spoken of.
segments.s305-g56: سیاووش is the patient/subject of the passive clause. رد appears as a short postpositive word (here printed as an element following the name; historically it can be an epithet or marker — treated here as a modifier). کشته شد is the passive 'was killed' (کشته = 'killed/slain' + شد auxiliary). بی‌گناه is a postpositive adjective 'innocent' qualifying the subject.

sentences.s305-s45: Here there is sowing and here there is eating; here, too, are the men of the day of battle.
sentences.s305-s46: A time of warmth and pleasure passed for you; the flowers and tulips, the colour and beauty, have passed.
sentences.s305-s47: Winter and cold lie ahead, so that hands grow dejected upon the spears.
sentences.s305-s48: They have cast folds like clouds into the skirts (or laps), and our dwelling turns to stone.
sentences.s305-s21: On his father's side the royal line reaches as far as Kay Qobad; on his mother's side his lineage traces to Tur.
sentences.s305-s22: Among the kings of the world his head rises highest; by his fame his name is a crest for the throne.
sentences.s305-s23: A keen-winged eagle swift in the heart of the cloud; a valiant whale in the sea of water.
sentences.s305-s24: All are the guardians of the throne; foes and snares are glad because of (his) fortune.
sentences.s305-s57: When bad times close in, my heart will not accept the teacher's advice.
sentences.s305-s58: By God's command, at the hour of sleep I become like a star before the sun.
sentences.s305-s59: I will cross over a sea of cream, and entrust to you my army and kingdom.
sentences.s305-s60: May my tomb be a mute fortress; may neither king nor army behold me.
sentences.s305-s41: Heed the turning of the world; make no one but him the teacher of your heart.
sentences.s305-s42: For we are in a fortress and the plain is yours; your head is full of vengeance, your heart full of blood.
sentences.s305-s43: I still call it a mute paradise; my homeland has been restored and my crops are fulfilled.
sentences.s305-s44: Here I have treasure, and here an army; here a gem and here a helmet.
sentences.s305-s37: Not a single dwelling remained in the wilderness; in the realm nothing was left except desolate plains.
sentences.s305-s38: Of us no renown remained; there was nothing but hatred and the wounds of the sharp sword until the Resurrection.
sentences.s305-s39: The Creator of the world would not approve that we be turned to a twisted end by harm.
sentences.s305-s40: And if you are always a seeker of war, O noble one, your heart’s hatred will not rest for a single moment.
sentences.s305-s61: When the day of seeking vengeance comes to me, behold that time of arraying the army.
sentences.s305-s62: I will come and demand from you my vengeance; I will find my due everywhere I go.
sentences.s305-s63: And if you cast hatred out of your mind, you will charm this land with love.
sentences.s305-s64: I will open (reveal) in the treasure-trove crown and girdle — that very throne, dinars and jeweled cups.
sentences.s305-s33: Look — how many broad cities are filled with gardens, iwans, squares, and palaces.
sentences.s305-s34: This grudge became the seeking of ruin — the pretext of Siyâvash and Afrâsiâb.
sentences.s305-s35: In that same campaign the mounted warriors in battle were in body like elephants and in strength like whales.
sentences.s305-s36: They had no shroud except the lions' appetite; nor was even a head near their bodies.
sentences.s305-s53: I will put this assembly to the sword; I shall fall into your hands, a captive.
sentences.s305-s54: Do not suppose that this too will not be destroyed; there is no one who does not wear away.
sentences.s305-s55: A scion of the heads of kings, Zādsham — from the line of Fereydun and from the seed of Jam.
sentences.s305-s56: I possess divine knowledge and farr; that same just God is my helper.
sentences.s305-s1: The next day, when the sun rose from longing, it set a golden lamp upon the wheel of the sky.
sentences.s305-s2: A loud cry rose from the fortress; the king was filled with thought because of that speech.
sentences.s305-s3: At that instant the gates of the fortress opened again, and the hidden secret was laid bare.
sentences.s305-s4: He came from the fortress of Jahān with ten horsemen, wise, learned, and wealthy.
sentences.s305-s5: He advanced to the curtained vestibule of the chamber; renowned men stood at its foot.
sentences.s305-s6: After that Manūshān came with his retinue and led the wise Jahan forward.
sentences.s305-s7: When the wise man reached Khosrow's presence, his tear‑eyed steed vanished.
sentences.s305-s8: A wondrous martial fury remained within him for Jahan, and he took a large helmet off his head.
sentences.s305-s49: From every side that I call an army will come; you will not endure the revolutions of the sun and moon.
sentences.s305-s50: Or suppose you now imagine that in every battle the star of fate will bestow (favor) upon you.
sentences.s305-s51: Unless Heaven's design passes away, no one else will encounter your suffering.
sentences.s305-s52: If (even) now you say that the Turks will take China, I will seize and strike the sky down upon the earth.
sentences.s305-s9: When he came close to his lofty throne, he cried "Afreen" to him and took him to prayer.
sentences.s305-s10: Thus he said, "O renowned sovereign, may you always pass through the world in joy."
sentences.s305-s11: May our land and home be auspicious for you; may the heart and eye of your ill-wisher be uprooted.
sentences.s305-s12: May goodness and the pious always be joyful, and may God‑worshippers stretch out their hands before our land.
sentences.s305-s29: My weary heart, full of sorrow from these words, has sunk to one side, exhausted by sleep and by eating.
sentences.s305-s30: I did not kill him; an unclean demon carried him off and took from my heart the fear of the world's sovereign.
sentences.s305-s31: Fate supplied him a bad pretext and threw me into the inner clutch of wicked tales.
sentences.s305-s32: You are now wise and king, a patron who receives/welcomes pious people.
sentences.s305-s69: I will make your entire army rich, and I will give you a golden throne and a helmet.
sentences.s305-s70: May my resolve be your companion in every battle; in every assembly I will call you king.
sentences.s305-s71: If you turn away from my counsel, and if you go to fight with your ancestors,
sentences.s305-s72: When you return from this, make ready for battle; I, too, have prepared for war like a leopard.
sentences.s305-s17: Thus Kafrasiyab said to the king; he sat, full of sorrow and with his eyelashes full of water.
sentences.s305-s18: First I sent a greeting to the king; because of that burning grief in the king's heart there is the Turanian host.
sentences.s305-s19: For God be praised and I flee to Him for refuge; I have seen a child in this stronghold.
sentences.s305-s20: who will raise an army, make himself sovereign, and ride at the head of the horsemen.
sentences.s305-s65: Take Tūr — whom Fereydun did not give to Irāj — and do not keep any memory of hatred.
sentences.s305-s66: And if you take women from China and Ma-chin, it is permissible; know that by that counsel the heart is driven by desire.
sentences.s305-s67: Khorasan and Makran and the land lie before you; my happiness, whether small or great, is yours.
sentences.s305-s68: On the road that King Kāvus passed, I sent you as many troops as were needed.
sentences.s305-s13: May it become auspicious and come back again; they would always tell tales of goodness.
sentences.s305-s14: I will send a message from Afrasiab, if the king does not, because of that, take to haste.
sentences.s305-s15: When the king heard Jahan's speech, he ordered that a golden one be placed before the court.
sentences.s305-s16: They set it down beneath; the wise man sat and remembered his father's message.
sentences.s305-s25: The great ones, who are decked with crowns and jewels, are, on the face of the earth, inferior to you in my eyes.
sentences.s305-s26: More astonishing than the demon's base deed is that he will never do to us anything but harm.
sentences.s305-s27: Given that kindness and that truth, why has my heart turned toward lack?
sentences.s305-s28: For Siyavash, the son of King Kavus, was killed by my hand, though innocent.
